news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking scientific discoveries revealing the oldest wooden tools used by humans and novel quantum behavior with atoms spinning backward.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique du jour met en avant des découvertes scientifiques révolutionnaires qui révèlent les plus anciens outils en bois utilisés par l'homme, ainsi qu'un comportement quantique inédit chez des atomes tournant à l'envers.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas más destacadas de hoy se centran en descubrimientos científicos revolucionarios que revelan las herramientas de madera más antiguas utilizadas por el ser humano y un novedoso comportamiento cuántico en el que los átomos giran al revés.

articles.title: Scientists discover the oldest wooden tools ever used by humans
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered the oldest known hand-held wooden tools ever used by humans — and they’re an astonishing 430,000 years old. Buried for hundreds of thousands of years at an ancient lakeside site in Greece, the carefully carved wooden objects reveal that early humans were far more skilled and resourceful than once believed.
articles.content:
Researchers have uncovered the oldest known hand-held wooden tools ever discovered, offering new insight into the skills and behavior of early humans nearly half a million years ago.

An international team led by scientists from the University of Reading, the University of Tübingen, and the Senckenberg Nature Research Society identified the ancient artifacts at the Marathousa 1 archaeological site in central Greece's Peloponnese region. The findings date back around 430,000 years.

The study, published in the journal PNAS, describes two carefully worked wooden objects that were shaped and used by humans. One was made from alder wood, while the other came from either willow or poplar. Researchers say the discovery pushes back evidence for this type of wooden tool use by at least 40,000 years.

The site also contained stone tools along with the remains of elephants and other animals, suggesting the area was once used for butchering prey near the edge of an ancient lake. Early humans occupied the site during the Middle Pleistocene, a period spanning roughly 774,000 to 129,000 years ago.

"The Middle Pleistocene was a critical phase in human evolution, during which more complex behaviors developed. The earliest reliable evidence of the targeted technological use of plants also dates from this period," says Professor Katerina Harvati, a paleoanthropologist and expert in human evolution, who leads the long-term research program at Marathousa 1.

Ancient Humans Worked Wood With Surprising Skill

Previous discoveries at the site, including stone and bone artifacts, had already shown that the people living there carried out a wide range of activities with considerable skill. Because of this, the research team decided to closely examine pieces of preserved wood recovered during excavations.

"Unlike stones, wooden objects need special conditions to survive over long periods of time," says Dr. Annemieke Milks, a leading expert in early wooden tools. "We examined all the wooden remains closely, looking at their surfaces under microscopes. We found marks from chopping and carving on two objects -- clear signs that early humans had shaped them."

One of the wooden artifacts was a small section of an alder branch or trunk that displayed clear shaping marks as well as signs of wear from use. Researchers believe it may have been used for digging in soft ground near the lakeshore or possibly for stripping bark from trees.

The second artifact was a much smaller piece of willow or poplar wood that also showed evidence of carving and possible use by humans.

Predator Claw Marks Reveal a Dangerous Environment

Not every marked piece of wood at the site was shaped by people. Researchers also studied a larger alder fragment with grooves carved into its surface. After detailed analysis, they concluded the marks had been left by a large carnivore, possibly a bear, rather than humans.

"The oldest wooden tools come from places such as the United Kingdom, Zambia, Germany, and China and include weapons, digging sticks, and tool handles. However, they are all more recent than our finds from Marathousa 1," Annemieke Milks says. There is only one older piece of evidence of wood used by humans, from the Kalambo Falls site in Zambia, dating to around 476,000 years ago. Yet that wood was used not as a tool but as structural material.

"We have discovered the oldest wooden tools known to date, as well as the first evidence of this kind from southeastern Europe," Harvati says. "This shows once again how exceptionally good the conditions at the Marathousa 1 site are for preservation. And the fact that large carnivores left their mark near the butchered elephant alongside human activity indicates fierce competition between the two."

The international research team also included scientists from the Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, the University of Ioannina, the Hellenic Ministry of Culture, and the American School of Classical Studies at Athens.

Research at the Marathousa 1 site was funded by the European Research Council and the German Science Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant archaeological discovery of the oldest known wooden tools used by humans, dating back 430,000 years. This finding provides meaningful new insights into early human technological skills and behavior, with broad significance for understanding human evolution. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a breakthrough in knowledge about ancient human tool use.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find oldest wooden tools made by humans, 430,000 years old
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study ancient tools to learn how early humans lived and used resources. Wooden tools are rare finds because wood usually decays over time.

**What happened** – A group of researchers found two wooden tools at a site in Greece called Marathousa 1. These tools are about 430,000 years old, making them the oldest wooden hand-held tools ever discovered. One tool was made from alder wood and might have been used for digging or stripping bark. The other was made from willow or poplar wood and also showed signs of careful shaping and use.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that early humans were skilled at making and using wooden tools much earlier than previously known. The site also had stone tools and animal bones, suggesting early humans hunted and prepared food there. Finding wooden tools this old helps us understand how people adapted and survived in their environment during the Middle Pleistocene period.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying the Marathousa 1 site to learn more about how early humans made tools and lived. They may also search for more wooden tools at other ancient sites to see if early humans used wood in different ways.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists uncovered the oldest wooden tools ever used by humans, revealing advanced skills nearly half a million years ago in Greece.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent les plus anciens outils en bois fabriqués par des humains, vieux de 430 000 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les outils anciens pour comprendre comment vivaient les premiers humains et utilisaient les ressources. Les outils en bois sont rares car le bois se décompose souvent avec le temps.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs a découvert deux outils en bois sur un site en Grèce appelé Marathousa 1. Ces outils ont environ 430 000 ans, ce qui en fait les plus anciens outils en bois portables jamais trouvés. L’un était en aulne et aurait servi à creuser ou à enlever l’écorce. L’autre, en saule ou peuplier, montre aussi des signes de façonnage et d’usage précis.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les premiers humains fabriquaient et utilisaient des outils en bois bien plus tôt qu’on ne le pensait. Le site contenait aussi des outils en pierre et des os d’animaux, ce qui laisse penser que les humains chassaient et préparaient leur nourriture là-bas. Trouver des outils en bois aussi anciens aide à mieux comprendre comment ils s’adaptaient et survivaient pendant la période du Pléistocène moyen.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d’étudier le site de Marathousa 1 pour en savoir plus sur la fabrication d’outils et la vie des premiers humains. Ils chercheront aussi d’autres outils en bois sur d’autres sites anciens pour voir comment le bois était utilisé.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert les plus anciens outils en bois fabriqués par des humains, montrant des compétences avancées il y a presque un demi-million d’années en Grèce.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran las herramientas de madera más antiguas hechas por humanos, con 430,000 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian herramientas antiguas para entender cómo vivían y usaban los recursos los primeros humanos. Las herramientas de madera son difíciles de encontrar porque la madera se descompone con el tiempo.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de investigadores encontró dos herramientas de madera en un sitio en Grecia llamado Marathousa 1. Estas herramientas tienen unos 430,000 años, siendo las herramientas de madera portátiles más antiguas descubiertas hasta ahora. Una estaba hecha de aliso y pudo haber servido para cavar o quitar corteza. La otra, de sauce o álamo, también muestra señales de haber sido tallada y usada cuidadosamente.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo demuestra que los primeros humanos fabricaban y usaban herramientas de madera mucho antes de lo que se pensaba. En el sitio también encontraron herramientas de piedra y huesos de animales, lo que sugiere que los humanos cazaban y preparaban alimentos allí. Encontrar herramientas de madera tan antiguas ayuda a entender cómo se adaptaban y sobrevivían durante el Pleistoceno Medio.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando el sitio Marathousa 1 para aprender más sobre cómo los primeros humanos fabricaban herramientas y vivían. También buscarán más herramientas de madera en otros sitios antiguos para ver si usaban la madera de diferentes maneras.

**Frase clave** – Científicos descubrieron las herramientas de madera más antiguas usadas por humanos, mostrando habilidades avanzadas hace casi medio millón de años en Grecia.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two ancient wooden tools—a carved alder branch and a smaller willow or poplar stick—resting on soft earth near a calm lakeshore, with subtle silhouettes of large elephant bones and faint claw marks on nearby wood, all rendered in natural, muted colors to evoke the skillful craftsmanship and prehistoric environment of early humans 430,000 years ago.

articles.title: Scientists discover atoms suddenly spinning backward in quantum experiment
articles.summary: Scientists have directly watched angular momentum move through a crystal for the very first time — and discovered a bizarre twist along the way. Using ultra-powerful terahertz laser pulses, researchers triggered tiny atomic rotations inside a quantum material and found that the direction of rotation can unexpectedly flip as momentum is transferred. The strange reversal happens because of the crystal’s underlying symmetry, creating an almost impossible-sounding effect where two rotations combine into one spinning the opposite way.
articles.content:
An international team of researchers has directly observed how angular momentum moves through a crystal lattice for the first time, revealing an unexpected quantum effect that causes the direction of rotation to reverse. The discovery, made using intense terahertz laser pulses, gives scientists a new view into the fundamental origins of magnetism and could eventually help researchers better control advanced quantum materials.

The study was led by scientists from the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR), the Fritz Haber Institute of the Max Planck Society, and collaborators in Berlin, Dresden, Jülich, and Eindhoven. Their findings were published in Nature Physics.

A Longstanding Mystery About Magnetism

In physics, quantities such as energy, momentum, and angular momentum are conserved, meaning they cannot disappear or be created from nothing. Instead, they move between different parts of a system. Angular momentum is familiar in everyday life through spinning objects like bicycle wheels or merry-go-rounds, but at the atomic scale it is deeply connected to magnetism.

More than a century ago, Albert Einstein and Wander Johannes de Haas demonstrated that changing the magnetization of a material could physically cause it to rotate. Their famous experiment showed that magnetic and mechanical angular momentum are linked together. Since then, scientists have tried to understand exactly how angular momentum spreads through the internal structure of solids.

Now, researchers have directly watched that process unfold inside a crystal.

Powerful Lasers Reveal Hidden Atomic Motion

The team studied how angular momentum travels between lattice vibrations, which are coordinated motions of atoms inside a crystal. To observe this, the scientists used ultra-strong terahertz laser pulses to drive one vibration into a circular motion. A second ultrafast laser pulse then tracked how that motion interacted with another coupled vibration in the material.

During the experiment, the researchers observed something surprising. As angular momentum moved from one vibration to another, the direction of rotation flipped.

The effect comes from the rotational symmetry of the crystal lattice. In this system, certain rotational states are physically equivalent even when they spin in opposite directions. According to the researchers, the result acts as a direct quantum mechanical signature of angular momentum conservation inside solids.

A Strange "1 + 1 = −1" Quantum Effect

The material used in the experiment, bismuth selenide, displayed especially unusual behavior. The angular momenta tied to its lattice vibrations combined in a way that produced a new rotation moving at twice the frequency but in the opposite direction.

Researchers describe this as a kind of "1 + 1 = −1" effect. In physics, this phenomenon resembles an Umklapp process, where motion is effectively reversed because of the symmetry of the crystal structure. Although Umklapp processes are already known in other areas of condensed matter physics, this is the first experimental demonstration involving lattice angular momentum.

"I find it extraordinarily elegant how the laws of physics are directly dictated by the symmetries of nature," says Olga Minakova, doctoral researcher at the Fritz Haber Institute of the Max Planck Society and central experimental physicist of the study.

Sebastian Maehrlein, head of department at the Institute of Radiation Physics at HZDR, professor at TU Dresden, and leader of the study, adds: "For me, these are exceptionally exciting results. We have discovered something fundamentally new that will hopefully make its way into the textbooks."

Future Applications for Quantum Technologies

Beyond solving a longstanding physics question, the findings could also have practical implications. Researchers say the work may help scientists gain greater control over ultrafast processes in quantum materials, potentially contributing to future information technologies and next generation memory devices.

Participating institutions included the Fritz Haber Institute of the Max Planck Society (Berlin), Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf, TU Dresden, Forschungszentrum Jülich, and Eindhoven University of Technology (Netherlands).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding angular momentum in quantum materials, revealing a novel quantum effect with potential broad implications for future quantum technologies and information devices. The discovery advances fundamental physics knowledge and could impact next-generation technology development, benefiting society at large.
articles.personality_title: Scientists observe atoms suddenly spinning backward inside crystals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long known that angular momentum, which is the spinning motion seen in things like bicycle wheels, also happens at a tiny atomic level. This spinning is closely linked to magnetism. However, until now, no one had directly watched how this spinning moves inside crystals.

**What happened** – A team of scientists from Germany and the Netherlands used powerful terahertz laser pulses to make atoms inside a special crystal called bismuth selenide spin. They then watched how this spinning motion traveled and found something surprising: the direction of the spin suddenly flipped. This means the atoms started spinning backward, an effect caused by the crystal’s unique symmetrical structure.

**Impact** – This is the first time researchers have seen angular momentum move and reverse inside a solid material. The discovery helps explain a mystery about how magnetism works at the atomic level. It also shows a rare quantum effect where two rotations combine to make one spin in the opposite direction, something scientists describe like “1 + 1 = -1.” This finding could help improve how we control quantum materials, which are important for future technology.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to use this new understanding to develop better quantum devices, such as faster computers and advanced memory storage. The research team plans to explore how to control these spinning motions more precisely to build new technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have watched atoms inside crystals suddenly spin backward, revealing a new quantum effect that could improve future technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques observent des atomes tournant soudainement à l'envers dans les cristaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques savent depuis longtemps que le moment angulaire, c’est-à-dire le mouvement de rotation comme celui des roues de vélo, existe aussi à l’échelle atomique. Ce mouvement est lié au magnétisme. Mais jusqu’à présent, personne n’avait observé directement comment ce mouvement se déplace à l’intérieur des cristaux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs d’Allemagne et des Pays-Bas a utilisé des impulsions laser terahertz très puissantes pour faire tourner les atomes dans un cristal spécial appelé séléniure de bismuth. Ils ont ensuite observé comment ce mouvement de rotation se propageait et ont découvert une surprise : la direction de la rotation s’est soudainement inversée. Cela signifie que les atomes ont commencé à tourner à l’envers, un effet causé par la structure symétrique unique du cristal.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des chercheurs voient le moment angulaire se déplacer et s’inverser dans un solide. Cette découverte aide à comprendre un mystère sur le fonctionnement du magnétisme au niveau atomique. Elle montre aussi un effet quantique rare où deux rotations se combinent en une seule rotation dans le sens opposé, décrit comme un effet « 1 + 1 = -1 ». Cette avancée pourrait aider à mieux contrôler les matériaux quantiques, importants pour les technologies futures.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent utiliser cette nouvelle compréhension pour développer de meilleurs dispositifs quantiques, comme des ordinateurs plus rapides et des mémoires avancées. L’équipe prévoit d’étudier comment contrôler plus précisément ces mouvements de rotation pour créer de nouvelles technologies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des scientifiques ont observé des atomes dans des cristaux tourner soudainement à l’envers, révélant un nouvel effet quantique qui pourrait améliorer les technologies futures.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos observan átomos girando repentinamente hacia atrás dentro de cristales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos saben desde hace tiempo que el momento angular, que es el movimiento de giro como el de las ruedas de una bicicleta, también ocurre a nivel atómico. Este giro está relacionado con el magnetismo. Sin embargo, hasta ahora nadie había visto directamente cómo se mueve este giro dentro de los cristales.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores de Alemania y los Países Bajos usó pulsos láser terahertz muy potentes para hacer girar átomos dentro de un cristal especial llamado seleniuro de bismuto. Luego observaron cómo se transmitía ese movimiento de giro y encontraron algo sorprendente: la dirección del giro cambió de repente. Esto significa que los átomos comenzaron a girar hacia atrás, un efecto causado por la estructura simétrica única del cristal.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos ven cómo el momento angular se mueve y se invierte dentro de un sólido. Este descubrimiento ayuda a entender un misterio sobre cómo funciona el magnetismo a nivel atómico. También muestra un efecto cuántico raro donde dos giros se combinan para formar uno que gira en la dirección opuesta, algo que los científicos llaman “1 + 1 = -1.” Este hallazgo podría ayudar a mejorar el control de materiales cuánticos, que son importantes para tecnologías futuras.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan usar este nuevo conocimiento para desarrollar mejores dispositivos cuánticos, como computadoras más rápidas y memorias avanzadas. El equipo planea investigar cómo controlar estos movimientos de giro con más precisión para crear nuevas tecnologías.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, los científicos han observado átomos dentro de cristales girando repentinamente hacia atrás, revelando un nuevo efecto cuántico que podría mejorar la tecnología futura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of intertwined, glowing crystal lattice structures with elegant, circular arrows flowing through them—one set of arrows spinning gently clockwise and another set spinning counterclockwise in a harmonious, mirrored dance—symbolizing the quantum reversal of atomic angular momentum inside a bismuth selenide crystal under soft terahertz light pulses.
